characters.blurb: Led the Senones that crushed Rome at the Allia and held the city for ransom until Camillus returned as dictator.
Rome’s rich rival across the Tiber. Camillus took it after a long siege—by tunnel into Juno’s temple, in the famous tale.

characters.relation: Goddess brought from Veii to Rome
characters.blurb: Juno Regina nodded (they say) when asked to go to Rome; Camillus’s evocatio made her a Roman goddess.
Heard the sacred geese and held the Tarpeian rock against the Gauls; later courted the debtors and fell as a demagogue.
Title of Marcus Manlius after defending the Capitol—glory that later curdled into sedition against Camillus’s order.
When their schoolmaster betrayed the boys to Camillus, he returned the traitor unbound and won the city by justice.
Marcus Furius Camillus—five times dictator—the man Plutarch paints as a second Romulus after the Gallic sack.
After the sack some urged abandoning Rome; Camillus argued like a re-founder that the site itself was sacred.
Stream where the Gauls destroyed the Roman army on a dies ater still marked in the calendar.
Etruscan-facing town taken and retaken in the wars; Camillus restored it in a single day’s hard fighting.

characters.relation: City of his exile; base of return
characters.blurb: Camillus was living in exile there when the Gauls came; from Ardea he gathered strength to relieve Rome.
Carried word through the enemy to the Capitol—and brought the Senate’s recall of Camillus as dictator.
Plebeian who reported a divine voice in the Via Nova foretelling the Gallic onset—ignored until too late.

characters.relation: Clan prominent in the Veientine war
characters.blurb: Their private war at the Cremera and later commands frame the generation Camillus dominated.
Tribune or commander who weighed the thousand pounds of gold before Camillus forbade the shameful bargain.
Pressed Rome while she was weak; Camillus’s dictatorships repeatedly broke their invasions.
Fought beside Volscians in the post-Gallic wars Camillus was called to master.
Broader Etruscan front—Capena, Falerii, and others—against which Camillus earned his early fame.
Several Lucii share Camillus’s Fasti as consular tribunes and fellow generals in the long Veientine struggle.
Named among Camillus’s awkward colleagues when politics cut short a command or triumph.

characters.relation: Peaceful king invoked after the sack
characters.blurb: When Romans debated moving to Veii, Camillus’s speech leaned on Numa’s religious founding of the city’s sites.

characters.relation: Town whose quarrel brought the Gauls
characters.blurb: Etruscan city that invited Roman envoys against the Gauls—and so drew Brennus south on Rome.
Lucumo wronged at Clusium who guided the Gallic mercenaries into Etruria—and toward Rome.
